ingredients.name: Term as an Indexed Value
ingredients.description: Each entry in a sequence has a position (index) and a value tied to that position.

ingredients.name: First Differences Reveal Change
ingredients.description: Subtracting consecutive terms exposes how the sequence grows from step to step.

ingredients.name: Constant vs Growing Difference
ingredients.description: A constant first difference signals linear growth; a changing difference signals a higher-order pattern.

ingredients.name: Predict Next Term From the Rule
ingredients.description: Once the difference pattern is known, the next term is produced by extending that rule.

ingredients.name: Ratio as a Comparison of Quantities
ingredients.description: A ratio expresses how two quantities relate by division.

ingredients.name: Unit Rate Normalizes to One
ingredients.description: A unit rate re-expresses a ratio so one quantity equals a single unit (per hour, per item).

ingredients.name: Rates Can Be Inverted
ingredients.description: Hours-per-room and rooms-per-hour are reciprocals describing the same work.

ingredients.name: Unit Conversion as a Chain of Ratios
ingredients.description: Converting units multiplies by ratios equal to one until the target unit remains.

ingredients.name: Proportion as Two Equal Ratios
ingredients.description: A proportion sets two ratios equal and can be solved for an unknown quantity.

ingredients.name: Signed Integer Arithmetic
ingredients.description: Reliable addition, subtraction, multiplication and division with negative numbers underpins all equation work.

ingredients.name: Like Terms Share Structure
ingredients.description: Terms with the same variable part can be combined because they represent the same kind of quantity.

ingredients.name: Inverse Operations Isolate the Unknown
ingredients.description: Operations are reversed in the opposite order to isolate the variable.

ingredients.name: Distribution Preserves Value
ingredients.description: Multiplying across parentheses rewrites an expression without changing its value.

ingredients.name: Move Variable Terms Across the Equals Sign
ingredients.description: When variables sit on both sides, terms are gathered to one side while preserving equality.

ingredients.name: Divisibility as Exact Division
ingredients.description: One number divides another when the division leaves no remainder.

ingredients.name: Prime as Indivisible Building Block
ingredients.description: A prime has exactly two factors: one and itself.

ingredients.name: Factors Come in Pairs
ingredients.description: Factors pair around the square root, which is why search stops at sqrt(n).

ingredients.name: Perfect Squares Have a Repeated Factor
ingredients.description: A perfect square has an integer square root, giving an odd factor count.

ingredients.name: Counting Factors
ingredients.description: The number of factors follows from the pairing structure and prime composition.

ingredients.name: Mean as Total Divided by Count
ingredients.description: The arithmetic mean is the sum of values divided by how many there are.

ingredients.name: Recover the Total From the Mean
ingredients.description: Multiplying mean by count reconstructs the total, enabling back-solving.

ingredients.name: Weighted Average Weights by Frequency
ingredients.description: A weighted average multiplies each value by its weight before dividing by total weight.

ingredients.name: Translate Word Problems Into Average Form
ingredients.description: Identifying which quantities are values, counts, and weights turns prose into an average computation.

ingredients.name: Name the Unknown as a Variable
ingredients.description: The quantity to find is assigned a symbol so relationships can be written about it.

ingredients.name: Translate Verbal Phrases to Operations
ingredients.description: Words like 'more than', 'product of', 'twice' map to specific arithmetic operations.

ingredients.name: Assemble Phrases Into an Equation
ingredients.description: Linked phrases combine into a single equation expressing the problem's constraint.

ingredients.name: Inequality Describes a Range
ingredients.description: An inequality specifies a set of values rather than a single solution.

ingredients.name: Open vs Closed Endpoints
ingredients.description: Strict inequalities exclude the endpoint (open); non-strict include it (closed).

ingredients.name: Number Line Represents the Solution Set
ingredients.description: Shading a number line shows which values satisfy the inequality.

ingredients.name: Compound Inequalities Combine Ranges
ingredients.description: Compound inequalities intersect or union ranges to describe bounded regions.

ingredients.name: Interval Notation Encodes the Range
ingredients.description: Brackets and parentheses encode endpoints and inclusion in compact form.

ingredients.name: Similarity Preserves Shape Not Size
ingredients.description: Similar figures have equal angles and proportionally scaled sides.

ingredients.name: Identify Corresponding Sides and Angles
ingredients.description: Corresponding parts must be matched correctly before any ratio is written.

ingredients.name: Scale Factor Is the Common Ratio
ingredients.description: The constant ratio between corresponding sides is the scale factor.

ingredients.name: Similarity Criteria (AA, SSS, SAS)
ingredients.description: Specific angle/side conditions are sufficient to guarantee similarity.

ingredients.name: Set a Proportion to Find a Missing Side
ingredients.description: Equating corresponding-side ratios produces a solvable proportion.

ingredients.name: Substitution Evaluates an Expression
ingredients.description: Replacing variables with values produces a numerical result.

ingredients.name: Exponent Rules Govern Powers
ingredients.description: Products, quotients, and powers of powers follow fixed exponent laws.

ingredients.name: Square of a Binomial Pattern
ingredients.description: (a+b)^2 expands to a^2+2ab+b^2, not a^2+b^2.

ingredients.name: Difference of Squares Pattern
ingredients.description: a^2-b^2 factors as (a+b)(a-b), a common answer-choice trap.

ingredients.name: Equivalent Forms Share a Value
ingredients.description: Two expressions are equivalent if they yield the same value for every input.

ingredients.name: Area Counts Unit Coverage
ingredients.description: Area measures how many unit squares cover a region.

ingredients.name: Square Area Is Side Squared
ingredients.description: A square's area is the side length multiplied by itself.

ingredients.name: Recover Side Length From Area
ingredients.description: Taking the square root of the area returns the side length.

ingredients.name: Pythagorean Relationship Links the Sides
ingredients.description: In a right triangle the squared legs sum to the squared hypotenuse.

ingredients.name: Diagonal Is the Hypotenuse
ingredients.description: A square's diagonal is the hypotenuse of the right triangle formed by two sides.

ingredients.name: Special Right Triangle Ratios
ingredients.description: 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 triangles have fixed side ratios.

ingredients.name: Coordinate Plane Locates Points
ingredients.description: An ordered pair fixes a point by horizontal and vertical position.

ingredients.name: Slope as Rise Over Run
ingredients.description: Slope measures vertical change divided by horizontal change between two points.

ingredients.name: Slope Formula From Two Points
ingredients.description: Slope equals the difference of y-values over the difference of x-values.

ingredients.name: Sign of Slope Shows Direction
ingredients.description: Positive slope rises, negative slope falls, zero is flat.

ingredients.name: Slope Is a Rate of Change
ingredients.description: Slope expresses how one quantity changes per unit of another.

ingredients.name: Rectangle Area Is Length Times Width
ingredients.description: A rectangle's area is the product of its two dimensions.

ingredients.name: Composite Area by Decomposition
ingredients.description: Irregular regions are split into rectangles whose areas add.

ingredients.name: Coverage Count by Dividing Areas
ingredients.description: Dividing total area by tile area gives the number of tiles needed.

ingredients.name: Area Unit Conversion Squares the Factor
ingredients.description: Converting area units squares the linear conversion factor.

ingredients.name: Identify Opposite, Adjacent, Hypotenuse
ingredients.description: Relative to a chosen acute angle, each side takes a named role.

ingredients.name: SOH-CAH-TOA Ratios
ingredients.description: Sine, cosine, and tangent are fixed ratios of the named sides.

ingredients.name: Cosine as Adjacent Over Hypotenuse
ingredients.description: Cosine compares the adjacent side to the hypotenuse for the chosen angle.

ingredients.name: Reciprocal Trig Functions
ingredients.description: Secant, cosecant, and cotangent are reciprocals of cosine, sine, and tangent.

ingredients.name: Formula Relates Multiple Variables
ingredients.description: A literal equation expresses a relationship among several symbols.

ingredients.name: Isolate the Target Variable
ingredients.description: Inverse operations isolate one chosen variable while treating others as constants.

ingredients.name: Factor to Isolate a Shared Variable
ingredients.description: When the target appears in multiple terms, factoring it out is required to isolate it.

ingredients.name: Divide by a Coefficient or Expression
ingredients.description: Isolation often ends by dividing both sides by a coefficient or grouped expression.

ingredients.name: Function Maps Inputs to Outputs
ingredients.description: A function assigns exactly one output to each input.

ingredients.name: Function Notation Names the Rule
ingredients.description: Notation like f(x) names the rule and the input being supplied.

ingredients.name: Substitute a Value Into a Function
ingredients.description: Evaluating replaces the input symbol everywhere it appears.

ingredients.name: Quotient of Function Values
ingredients.description: Some problems evaluate two functions and divide the results.

bridges.description: Knowing how to isolate an unknown lets a student trust that a translated equation is solvable.
A translated equation frequently lands with variables on both sides, requiring linear-solving machinery to finish.
Phrases like 'twice the sum of' produce parenthesised expressions that must be distributed.
Isolating a numeric unknown generalises to isolating a chosen symbol among other symbols.
Comfort distributing in reverse is what makes factoring a shared variable out feel natural.
A scale factor is a unit-rate comparison between corresponding sides.
Solving a generic proportion is the exact mechanic used to find a missing similar-triangle side.
Slope is a ratio of vertical to horizontal change; ratio reasoning underpins it.
Interpreting slope as a rate of change reuses unit-rate thinking.
Dividing total area by tile area is a unit-rate / conversion computation in disguise.
Unit-coverage intuition is the basis for the rectangle area formula.
Squaring a side length is an application of exponent rules.
Expanding squared sums supports algebraic work on diagonal lengths.
Knowing the side relationships of a right triangle is needed before naming opposite/adjacent/hypotenuse.
Special-triangle ratios give exact cosine values for common angles.
Evaluating an expression by substitution is the same act as evaluating a function at an input.
Recognising equivalent forms helps simplify quotients of function values.
A constant first difference IS a constant rate of change, the conceptual seed of slope.
Expressing a sequence rule symbolically is a gateway to writing equations from patterns.
Recognising perfect squares is prerequisite to spotting a difference of two squares.
Factor-pair thinking supports factoring a^2-b^2 into a product.
Solving inequalities reuses inverse operations, with a sign-flip caveat on multiplication by negatives.

combinations.note: distributive_rewrite and like_terms_share_structure are siblings (both children of integer_arithmetic_fluency) with no edge between them; the real solving order distributes BEFORE combining, the reverse of the dependency order. A dependency backtrack from variables_both_sides reaches the two simplification skills only partially.

combinations.example_problem: Twice a number increased by 4 equals three times the number decreased by 7. Find the number.
combinations.note: Translation ingredients live in words_to_equations; the actual solving ingredients live in linear_equations. Only one bridge connects the two concepts, so the full fire-set spanning both is invisible to a single backtrack.

combinations.example_problem: Two similar triangles have a 6 cm side corresponding to a 9 cm side. Find the side corresponding to the 8 cm side.
combinations.note: Finishing the proportion requires inverse_operations_isolate from linear_equations, which has no dependency or bridge into proportion_solves_missing_side. The geometry chain stops short of the algebra that closes the problem.

combinations.example_problem: A square has area 50. Find the length of its diagonal.
combinations.note: side_from_area and pythagorean_relationship are siblings under different parents and never co-linked; a diagonal problem must combine recovering the side, then applying Pythagoras / the 45-45-90 ratio. No single edge ties area-inversion to the diagonal step.

combinations.example_problem: A right triangle has hypotenuse 13 and one leg 5. Find the cosine of the angle adjacent to the known leg.
combinations.note: pythagorean_relationship is in squares_area_right_triangles while the trig ratio is in right_triangle_trig; the bridge runs the other direction (geometry enables trig) but the problem fires them jointly to get the missing side first, then the ratio.

combinations.example_problem: A tank holds 12 L at minute 2 and 30 L at minute 8. At what rate is it filling, in litres per minute?
combinations.note: The problem fuses coordinate-slope computation with rates_proportion unit-rate interpretation. These live in separate concepts joined by a single weak bridge; the combination, not the bridge, is what the problem needs.

combinations.example_problem: A student's mean over 5 tests is 82. After a 6th test the mean is 84. What was the 6th score?
combinations.note: Back-solving from the mean fires total reconstruction plus an inverse-operation isolation step from linear_equations, across a concept boundary with no bridge.

combinations.example_problem: A class of 20 averages 70 and a class of 30 averages 80. What is the combined average?
combinations.note: Weighted averaging silently uses ratio_as_comparison from rates_proportion to weight by class size. That cross-concept reliance is never edged.

combinations.example_problem: Evaluate which answer choice equals (x+3)^2 - (x^2+9) for all x.
combinations.note: The distractor structure requires holding square_of_binomial and difference_of_squares simultaneously to reject traps. Both are siblings under exponent_rules with no co-edge; recognising the trap is a combination, not a prerequisite chain.

combinations.example_problem: Solve for r:  A = P + Prt.
combinations.note: Isolating r requires factoring it out of two terms then dividing by the grouped expression — combining literal-equation ingredients with reverse-distribution from linear_equations. The factoring-to-isolate move is exactly where students stall and is only partially bridged.

combinations.example_problem: A floor is 12 ft by 9 ft. Tiles are sold in square yards. How many square yards of tile are needed?
combinations.note: Mixing area in feet with tiles in yards forces area_unit_conversion (square the factor) plus unit_conversion_chains from rates_proportion. The squared-factor pitfall lives at the junction of two concepts.

combinations.example_problem: If f(x)=x^2+1 and g(x)=x+1, find f(3)/g(3).
combinations.note: Evaluating polynomial function values pulls exponent_rules from expression_forms into functions_evaluation; the quotient step then combines two independent evaluations. Neither cross-use is a dependency.

combinations.example_problem: A sequence is 4, 7, 10, 13, ... Write a rule for the nth term and find the 20th term.
combinations.note: Going from a constant difference to a closed-form linear rule fuses sequence reasoning, slope-as-rate, and equation writing across three concepts. The pattern-to-formula leap is a combination the DAG cannot sequence.

combinations.example_problem: How many positive integers less than 100 have an odd number of factors?
combinations.note: The insight (only perfect squares have an odd factor count) requires combining factor-pairing with perfect-square structure and counting. These are within one concept but the combination is the actual problem, not any single prerequisite.

combinations.example_problem: Solve -3x + 5 < 14 and graph the solution, then write it in interval notation.
combinations.note: Solving reuses inverse_operations_isolate from linear_equations (with a sign-flip), then represents the range on a line and in interval notation. The algebra and the representation halves live in different concepts joined by one bridge; the problem needs the full fused set.
